title: Platform Engineering for Everyone - Success Can’t Be Coded
gist: Max Körbächer argues internal development platforms fail not on technology but on missing product mindset, purpose, user research, and measurement; he prescribes treating platforms as evolving products, using DevEx and SPACE metrics over DORA, and warns that AI, hype cycles, and sunk-cost thinking repeatedly break platform efforts.

insights.claim: Internal development platforms fail primarily because of missing purpose, absent vision, unmeasured user needs, and infrastructure-first thinking, not because of technology shortcomings.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: For anyone evaluating platform-engineering tooling vendors, the binding constraint is organizational, so tool-centric pitches are a red flag.
insights.quote: the success of platforms isn't in its code
insights.locator: Final Note on Platform Success

insights.claim: Companies typically start with a developer portal like Backstage rather than a platform, chasing the attractive UI, then burn five engineers and months of effort configuring extensions before seeing expected value.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Backstage adoption often destroys ROI early; buyers should treat portal-first strategies skeptically and question Backstage's cost-to-value.
insights.quote: you need five engineers and a couple of months, and then it's like, burn money like hell
insights.locator: Why Internal Development Platforms Tend to Fail

insights.claim: Infrastructure-first thinking is the biggest killer of platforms because most platform engineers come from cloud/infrastructure backgrounds and design the tech stack before considering the person who will use it.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Team composition (infra-heavy, no product/UX skills) predicts platform failure; a diligence signal when assessing platform teams.
insights.quote: The biggest killer itself is the infrastructure-first thinking
insights.locator: Why infrastructure-first thinking might be a problem

insights.claim: Körbächer coins 'randomeering'—adding tools to a platform simply because an engineer saw them at a conference like KubeCon—as a common driver of over-engineered, unnecessary platform complexity.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Tool sprawl driven by hype adds cost without solving problems; a governance principle can curb it.
insights.quote: randomeering. I don't know what the term is called, but it's a mix of random and engineering
insights.locator: My personal favorite is randomeering

insights.claim: Large enterprises cycle repeatedly between harmonization and fragmentation, with each reorganization and industry hype wave (IoT, cloud-native, now AI) breaking apart standardized environments and driving high staff rotation.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Explains why enterprise tooling standardization is perpetually undone; vendors selling harmonization face a recurring but unstable demand.
insights.quote: you have some time where you have harmonization... and then comes a reorganization, and shit goes down
insights.locator: New Tech Stacks - Shift Left, Adoption, and Duplicated Efforts

insights.claim: Körbächer asserts current AI/ML/LLM adoption is failing more than succeeding, wasting more money than it delivers, citing 'the latest studies.'
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: A skeptical practitioner view on enterprise AI ROI; a counterweight to bullish AI-adoption narratives when sizing the market.
insights.quote: Look at the latest studies. You can see the story. It's failing. It's more failing than being successful.
insights.locator: Nowadays, AI, machine learning, LLMs

insights.claim: Körbächer notes IoT was hyped a decade ago as the next revolution, still exists but delivered no revolution, offering a cautionary template for current AI hype.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Pattern-match: hype cycles rarely deliver the promised revolution on schedule; useful discipline against AI over-extrapolation.
insights.quote: IoT is still there, but where's the revolution? I don't know.
insights.locator: Ten years back, everyone was IoT

insights.claim: Platforms must be run as evolving products, not projects, because a project has a deadline and dies when it ends, whereas a platform must continuously evolve and cannot be enforced onto developers.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Product-vs-project framing predicts whether a platform investment sustains; watch for dedicated product ownership as a success signal.
insights.quote: It's bad for a product.
insights.locator: Those projects, and I really hate that word

insights.claim: A platform without adoption is just infrastructure, and mandating usage by executive fiat backfires by increasing developer resistance rather than driving voluntary adoption.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Forced adoption metrics are misleading; genuine voluntary usage is the real value indicator for internal platforms.
insights.quote: a platform without any kind of adoption... is just infrastructure
insights.locator: For me, a platform without any kind of adoption

insights.claim: Platforms serve not only developers but security personnel, compliance officers, product owners and business people; one of Körbächer's most successful platforms has no fancy frontend yet nearly half of VPs check a GitHub report weekly.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Value can come from simple reporting to non-developer stakeholders, not slick UIs, reframing where platform investment pays off.
insights.quote: almost half of all VPs are looking at least once in a week somewhere in GitHub for a report about the platform
insights.locator: One of our most successful platforms

insights.claim: Körbächer recommends mixing 'we will' principles (building team unity, e.g. driving transparency of resource consumption) with 'you must' principles (best-practice guidance), while warning that overly strict musts become directives, not principles.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: A concrete governance framework for aligning platform decisions and reducing tool sprawl.
insights.locator: There are a few ways on how to do, actually, with the principles

insights.claim: Körbächer's FinOps team cut cloud costs by roughly 50% at every company they entered simply by focusing on eliminating waste, and a platform built right from the start keeps costs aligned to demand without needing later cuts.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: A repeatable ~50% cloud cost reduction signals large latent overspend in enterprises, the core opportunity for FinOps tooling.
insights.quote: every company we went into, we could cut costs by 50% just because we are there
insights.locator: In former times, my team did FinOps

insights.claim: Analyst reports are pay-to-play: no vendor lands in a report without paying, so Körbächer treats analyst-driven tool selection as his most-hated but still influential driver.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Discount analyst placements as objective signal; vendor presence reflects marketing spend, not product quality.
insights.quote: No provider on this planet is ending up in a report because they have not paid for it.
insights.locator: Then, obviously, you have the data and insights for analysts

insights.claim: Personal drivers—engineers' ethical or moral objections to a tool's owners or a company's political orientation—can genuinely kill motivation and derail platform development, a factor companies avoid confronting.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Values-based tool resistance is a real adoption risk; relevant amid sovereignty and vendor-ethics debates.
insights.quote: I do not support the political orientation of where this company is located.
insights.locator: What you often underestimate is also personal drivers

insights.claim: European (especially German) digital sovereignty pressure is increasingly a market driver of tool selection, sometimes extending to scrutinizing whether an open-source project's contributors are located in Europe.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Sovereignty requirements are reshaping European enterprise procurement; a tailwind for EU-based/local tooling.
insights.quote: you have a lot of the sovereignty push at the moment that could also drive, for example, what are you using
insights.locator: Market drivers is like everything

themes.theme: Measurement: DORA vs SPACE vs DevEx

insights.claim: Companies routinely fail to measure platform success by starting measurement only after implementation, leaving no baseline to compare against, so Körbächer insists on measuring the pre-platform state first.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Without a pre-implementation baseline, claimed platform ROI is unverifiable; a due-diligence check on any value claim.
insights.quote: I cannot define the success of a platform if I start measuring how good it is after I've implemented it.
insights.locator: Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)

insights.claim: Körbächer is skeptical of DORA metrics because in management hands they become a weapon that pressures teams to ship faster, degrading quality, rather than a diagnostic tool.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: DORA-driven performance management can be counterproductive; a caution for orgs and vendors marketing DORA dashboards.
insights.quote: If you see that management get it into their hand, it's a weapon, and it usually stresses out teams.
insights.locator: What you will often see is like, use the DORA metrics

insights.claim: Körbächer prefers SPACE metrics (satisfaction, performance, activity, communication, efficiency) and DevEx flow metrics over DORA because they measure experience per person, team, or system and require correlating signals like review velocity to shipped code volume.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: SPACE/DevEx are the maturing measurement standards for platform value; watch tooling that operationalizes them.
insights.locator: I'm more a fan of the SPACE metrics

insights.claim: Numbers alone can lie because two correlated-looking metrics may be coincidental, so Körbächer insists quantitative platform metrics must be paired with direct, repeated in-person user feedback.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Pure-telemetry platform-analytics products are insufficient without qualitative feedback loops.
insights.quote: numbers can lie
insights.locator: Those are the things where you actively need to go into

insights.claim: For every 5 minutes of disruption, a person's brain can spend up to an hour still processing, so three 15-minute meetings impose cognitive load far beyond the 45 minutes of meeting time.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Quantifies the hidden productivity cost of meeting fragmentation, a lever for developer-experience improvement.
insights.quote: For every 5 minutes you are disrupted, you can add up up to one hour where your brain is still processing information.
insights.locator: Cognitive load, I would say almost an overloaded term

insights.claim: Once-a-year feedback surveys capture only a one-time emotional snapshot and are near-worthless; effective feedback must be continuous, product-team-owned, and use open questions rather than yes/no.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Continuous, team-owned feedback is the moat for platform improvement; annual enterprise surveys are noise.
insights.quote: Once in a year, how fantastic?
insights.locator: Implementing the feedback loop is hard

insights.claim: Körbächer recommends five user-research questions before building a platform—current tooling, current workflow, migration effort, existing gaps, and the ideal workflow—focused on functionality rather than specific tools.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: A concrete discovery checklist that separates real requirements from tool preferences.
insights.locator: For the user research, just to emphasize it more

insights.claim: Körbächer's core thesis is that platform problems are caused by people and processes, not technology itself.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Reinforces that sociotechnical, not technical, factors determine platform outcomes.
insights.quote: problems are caused because of people and processes and not because of the technology itself
insights.locator: The message here is like, problems are caused

insights.claim: The sunk-cost fallacy in enterprises is reinforced by accounting/tax rules that require using an investment until depreciation completes, forcing teams to keep useless tools running to avoid angering accountants.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Tax/accounting mechanics, not just psychology, lock in legacy tooling; explains slow enterprise tool churn.
insights.quote: you need to use it until you get all your taxes back
insights.locator: This is very hard because most companies have a problem

insights.claim: Körbächer prescribes defining depreciation criteria upfront—the point at which a platform feature is declared trash based on overwhelming subjective user feedback—as equally important as defining purpose and KPIs.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Pre-committed retirement criteria counter sunk-cost lock-in; a discipline for managing platform lifecycle cost.
insights.quote: you also need to define when something is trash
insights.locator: That's why you need depreciation criterias.

insights.claim: Kubernetes-based platforms accumulate significant technical debt within about a year due to constant maintenance, updates, and tools losing their contributor base and needing replacement.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: High ongoing maintenance cost is inherent to Kubernetes platforms; factor into TCO for platform bets.
insights.quote: after one year, a lot of technical debt
insights.locator: Because we are on Kubernetes

insights.claim: Tracing and reducing shadow IT is Körbächer's preferred proxy for platform desirability, evidenced by one Telco customer whose external IT-services spend dropped drastically once an internal platform solved even a few identified problems.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Falling shadow-IT and external-services spend is a measurable ROI signal for internal platforms.
insights.quote: the expenses for external IT services have drastically dropped
insights.locator: We have one larger Telco customer

insights.claim: Körbächer advocates starting with the 'thinnest viable platform'—one or two genuinely helpful features—then iterating feature by feature to limit technical debt and demonstrate continuous improvement.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Minimal-viable-platform sequencing reduces risk and makes ROI measurable; a build-discipline signal.
insights.quote: you start with the thinnest viable platform
insights.locator: The one way is to be very careful

insights.claim: Building a contributor community around a platform—inviting InnerSource and open-source contributions—is a lifesaver that raises both adoption and happiness, per Körbächer's most successful customers.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Community contribution is a durable adoption flywheel for internal platforms.
insights.locator: Build a community that's for the most customers

insights.claim: Körbächer recommends challenging Conway's Law by building a platform with an ideal workflow and adjusting the company to follow it, a multi-year change-management journey.
insights.speaker: Max Körbächer
insights.so_what: Reversing Conway's Law is high-effort but high-leverage; realistic only with multi-year commitment and change management.
insights.quote: Build a platform with an ideal workflow and adjust the company to follow the platform workflow.
insights.locator: Challenge Conway's Law.
